sentences.text: Yes, I-- I had a friend named William Holland.
And his mother had been related to the Stulpnagel family.
They were Germans and Americans of course.
He was very educated and very interesting, spoke perfect German, looked German.

And the only place in Paris that you had a decent meal, I guess it-- just after the occupation of the Germans was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where the German messes were, which they took over all the best restaurants and all.
And he knew a few of the Germans.
He wasn't a bit pro-German or anything, in the sense of, you know, he wasn't in any way-- didn't agree in any way with the Nazis.
But he used to come back and tell me all the things that he had heard from them, knowing that I was going to pass it on to some of my friends who were in the French.
What-- I didn't know what it was, but it was the underground.
And so we-- that's how I more or less got interested in that part of it.
And then one day I ran into a woman whom I knew quite well, and had known her at parties and all.
And we thought of her as a collaborator.
And I didn't offer to shake hands or anything, which was very stupid of me because it meant that I wasn't, you know, that I was anti-Nazi.
And you didn't want to be anti-Nazi at that moment.
Obviously.
Oh, can't remember.
I've got it written down somewhere in fact because she-- in an old address book, I found her name not long ago.
And she's the one who told me about if I would marry one of the girls in the camp.
And actually I did it by-- just by signing papers.
I didn't have to go to the camp or anything and do it.
Just outside of Paris.
I think beyond Lucien, or somewhere like that.
It was what the French-- which we call-- we learned later on were concentration . camps.
But it was a detention place for undesirable foreigners and things like that, which meant they were Jewish.
And I have to say that sometimes the French did very, very courageous things with-- with them.
I don't-- I don't think the picture of Vichy was-- was always the complete description of the French.
There-- there were lots of them, of course, who were.
There were two or three kinds of collaborators.
First place, the fifth column, you know, when there were retreats and all were dreadful.
They were doing everything they could to-- but they could have been Alsatian, or we don't know what, but doing everything they could to, you know, stir up problems and all.
But the-- it was the opportunist who wanted to get along because they knew the Germans were going to run things, and then the cowards who just did it because they were afraid.
And then I suppose there were some who thought, well, it's the lesser of the evils.
And we never discussed it with them, because we dodged these people as much as we could.
I couldn't possibly pass judgment or-- I don't know how they really felt because we never talked to them.
We dodged them as much as we could.
No, no.
No, no.
The minute the ambulance corps thing started, we no-- I--
Yes.
Oh, soon after the-- after the war started.
And we were attached to French units.
And most of them were my age, or maybe a little bit older.
Just driving an ambulance.
From-- to hospitals and-- and this more or less, you can imagine what ambulance work was like.
And I didn't know anything about-- I had studied first aid when I was in-- I had won a scholarship to a-- to the national aquatic school.
And it's mostly swimming and diving.
But we also learned first aid.
And so I knew a little bit about first aid.
Not that that helped in any way.
Probably what I did hurt more than helped.
Oh, well, when the-- when this episode happened where we went to Marseille with these soldiers.
Then I ran into a Jewish friend of mine there whom I'd known in Paris.
And I don't think I'd known him in Paris.
I'd known him in Brussels or somewhere.
But he-- I knew him from that period, you know.
And I didn't remember him well.
He remembered me more than I remembered him.
And we chatted away a little bit.
And he told me about this man Varian Fry.
And I went to-- we had a friend who worked very much and was very helpful to Varian named Mary Jane Gold.
And she and Miriam Davenport were very helpful to Fry.
Mary Jane had quite a bit of money, which she even gave to the organization.
And also she had a plane.
And I heard that she had the plane, and I thought, since I had studied aviation, that maybe I could make a shuttle, you know, to North Africa.
So I went to see her.
In fact, it's in-- some of that's in this book that she wrote.
And she told me about Fry.
And I went there.
And, of course, there's these wonderful people.
The people who-- I was an errand boy.
I wasn't a member of the-- of the brain trust.
There was Daniel Benedict, of course, who died and-- and Hirschman.
We called him Beamish, very, very brilliant and enormous help to Fry.
In fact, he was-- I think a lot of the, for example, the way over the mountains, he had been in the French-- in the Spanish Civil War.
He knew how to get over the mountains.
A lot of things that he did were brilliant.
And he had a lot of courage.
And he was Jewish, of course.
And then Jean Gemahling, who also was absolutely brilliant in underground things and all.
And-- and then Benedict, Daniel Benedict, he's the one who told me about the-- the gangsters, how to get in with the gangsters and all.
In Marseille.
They started an organization, a cover to-- American Secours-- to help people by giving them money and so forth.
And-- although they had a lot of trouble getting money.
Of course, we were volunteers.
But in fact, I think everybody in it were volunteers.
Maybe one or two secretaries had to be-- have their hotel paid.
But it was an organization to cover what we were doing, trying to smuggle these people out.
And--
Well, and then-- then I was in Marseille.

And I did one or two little things, like I met some friends who thought of a brilliant idea of getting a boat to take people out, and trying to get a British warship to pick them up, which was a pretty stupid idea because it was-- you know, we were kids.
We didn't-- everything we did was dumb.
And I had a friend-- friend, someone I knew, named Freddie McEvoy.
And he was living with a woman named Beatrice Cartwright, who had a beautiful yacht.
So I went there to see if I could maybe get them to help us with this project, which was pretty crackpot idea anyway.
And Beatrice said to me, oh, I looked terrible cause I hadn't had a bath and shave and all this.
And so I went and took a shower and everything.
I knew him quite well.
And so when Freddie came on board, I said-- we were very friendly.
And so we walked out on the deck, and I told him what we wanted to do.
And suddenly he had a nervous breakdown.
He said, get off the ship, you get off the boat, you're going to get me in trouble and all.
And of course, they heard this.
People on the docks heard it.
And needless to say, I got off.
But we had-- we had a few things-- plans to do things that didn't work.
That's why when I started working for Fry, actually you had to have-- he had to have somebody there.
I had-- still had remnants of my-- of my ambulance driver's uniform because it's all the clothes I had.
And it looked a little bit impressive to the refugees.
And at one point, some of them, they were trying-- you didn't know who was trying to get in, whether they were real refugees or not.
We didn't know.
And I was supposed to stand there at the head of the stairs and try and keep a little bit of order.
That was my first job there.
Then I started delivering papers, your false papers, and taking people.
And that-- because I knew the way to get out of the [INAUDIBLE], I'd go and pick them up and bring them into Marseille, and then finally down to the frontier, and different things.
Well, I have to tell you, I wasn't terribly impressed because he-- as I say, he had this little bow tie.
And he looked like a real dandy.
And I was thinking, is this the man that everybody's saying is so incredible?
And it seemed that every-- every Jew in France knew about him by now.
How he got away with it, nobody ever knows.
But I have the feeling that his looks had a lot to do with it, because they wouldn't take him seriously.
Well, let me tell you.
He was--
He was the most idealistic man I've ever met.
And-- and that's why if he thought we were trying to-- I mean, you know, it's been 50 years, hasn't it?
Practically none of my friends-- my wife knew none of this until the Holocaust Museum opened.
And then she read a couple of books on the subject.
But she didn't know any of it.
I never-- you know--
Fry would have been annoyed as hell if he thought we were trying to, you know--
They sent me.
This woman, Mary Jane Gold, told me where to go.
And I went to see-- first to see Miriam Davenport, who's now Miriam Ebel.
And she was an art expert and all.
And, you see, Fry was given the mission to rescue all these famous painters and artists and musicians and writers and people who were on-- way up on the list of enemies of the state.
And-- but if somebody was an artist, she used to send them down to the port to make a drawing and come back and show if they really were artists and all.
But I used to say to Fry, look, I met this nice old man and he's got his grandmother, and so and so.
Oh, put them on the list, put them on the list.
And then one of my jobs was going-- taking telegrams at night.
You had to go-- you had to have a stamp from either the prefect of police or the American consulate.
Well, the American consulate wasn't about to give us one.
But I knew a woman there who was the-- excuse me, I beg your pardon-- who was the receptionist.
Her name was Delapre, Madame Delapre.
Her-- she was the widow of the famous journalist who was thrown off the plane-- off the train in Spain during the Civil War there.
And I would go in and take a piece of her stationery and on a soft lead pencil I would write a telegram, urgent telegram, to my family.
Address, I don't know what address I put on anything.
And then she would stamp it.
And I would go out.
And we would erase the soft lead pencil and type in Mr.
And Mrs.
So and So must come to Marseille or to Bordeaux or wherever to regulate their papers, since they are American and so forth.
And we got about five or six before she caught on to it.
She never told on it.
Another time we had-- we weren't very popular with the-- but there was one wonderful man there, Harry Bingham.
He was a vice-consul.
And he helped a lot, everything that he could.
You know-- you know, he was told not to do anything.
And they, you know, they thought we were rocking the boat.
You know, after all they had to keep-- playing ball with Vichy.
They had to, I guess.
But we didn't see it that way.
No, just wherever-- and I changed a lot.
You had to change a lot.
And you had to find out first if the people were pro-Nazi.
And then I found a place in the Old Port.
That's how I really got to be friends with the gangsters.
By the way, that-- that friendship with the gangsters is what got me my first job in films.
One day, I'll-- not on camera, but I'll tell you about that.
That was a--
Yes, he said we need somebody who-- tough and all, who can sort out anybody who might be trying to make trouble in the office, you know.
And the office was American Secours, you know, trying to help, a charitable organization.
But underneath it, and with all the secret messages and everything, was this business of the-- and it went on and on.
How they got away with it, I will never know.
How we-- any of us got away with it, I don't know.
We had one policeman, Captain Dubois.
That sounds like-- that's like saying John Smith, you know.
But Captain Dubois was our friend.
And, in fact, he's the one who warned me that, eventually, that-- we were all arrested at different times.
You know, when I say arrested, we were detained.
We were questioned.
And Fry was put on a boat one time for about three days.
And with all his-- you know, as I say, the brain trust there was Daniel Benedict, Jean Gemahling and Hirschman-- Beamish, our friend Beamish.
He was brilliant, absolutely-- he's now at the-- at Princeton University.
He's an absolutely brilliant man.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Sometimes.
Sometimes.
Yes.
We had a few-- there was a wonderful bar there named the Citron-- Bar Citron.
And it had an entrance on the port and another one on a little square in the back.
And you could go out either way.
And we had two waiters there.
One who worked in the first end, one the other.
And neither one of them were Jewish, but they knew very well that we were doing something.
They didn't know what.
I think they thought we-- we were pro-British.
I think that's what it was.
But we would make, lots of times, meetings there.
And we would sit in the middle.
And if Germans came in one side-- you knew who the Germans were.
I mean, some of them were so stupid they even clicked their heels when they met you, you know what I mean.
You can't believe how stupid some of them were.
Thank god.
And they would tell us, and we'd we go out the other side, very casually of course.
But then we had a-- I used to have to take-- in Cassis, which is a little town outside, a seaport town.
I used to have to take messages there, false papers, and all.
And Madame Juliette, enormous, was the owner of this bar, Bar de la Marine, and that's where the German collaborators and all would come.
And she would come in, and take papers out, and plank them down on the table in front of us, the best way to do it.
If she'd come in and slipped them like this to us, somebody would have seen it.
She'd come in and plank them down like that.
Here are those things you wanted me to bring you.
And they were secret, false papers, and things that any one of them would have had us shot.
We would have both been taken out and shot, you know.
She was wonderful.
Yes, yes.
Quite small.
And-- and we found quite often if you talk to the right people, they would close their eyes.
For example, as I say, you know that challenge and response.
If you expect good of people, sometimes they do it, even the bad ones.
And I think that's why Fry got away with it, is because they would call him down, and, oh, they were going to put him in-- send him off right away.
I mean, not deport him.
They even threatened him with, you know, concentration camp even though he was an American.
See, he and I and Mary Jane and Miriam were all Americans.
And but the others were Jewish.
Well, of course, they were Jewish, too, but they-- they had American passports.
And that-- you know, that gave you a certain amount of protection.
But not always for Fry.
They knew that he was doing something, but they didn't know what.
And they were determined to find out.
And then he would go in and talk to them, and he was so innocent.
Those big eyes just sort of-- spectacles, not glasses, spectacles.
And he was always taking them out and cleaning, then putting them-- oh, he was the best actor I've ever seen.
There was no way on Earth-- no way on Earth that he would suspicion him of doing something dishonest.
Yeah, thank goodness.
Everybody loved him, I tell you.
Well, they would just take you down to question you.
And if you--
You never knew.
Oh, I-- later on, I was taken to-- when I finally left, I was told by-- this Captain Dubois warned us that I'd better go back that night, not even go to my hotel.
But we had already-- we'd taken some sculptures-- a sculpture that I'd done and hollowed it out.
And they put inside it a lot of secret messages that had to get out of the country.
And they knew that I was going to have to go, sooner or later, and probably sooner.
And as it turned out, it was.
And then we sealed it over.
And then also in my trumpet, you take out part of the third valve, the extension, and they put a lot of things in that and put it back.
And those are places you wouldn't expect people to investigate.
So then with my American passport-- I had another sort of a passport, a carte d'identite with another name too, but that I quickly destroyed.
And with-- I wasn't going over the mountains because that was already closed.
I was just going to go-- try to go out legally.
So I got to the frontier and I saw that the-- the guards-- as I say, when their suits were very well pressed and all, you knew they weren't French [INAUDIBLE].
And they were very polite-- almost clicking their heels.
I knew they were going to ask me, are you taking any papers out of the country or any secret messages or anything.
And I knew I would blush.
And because, you know, in school we were taught not to lie.
When you lie, you have to cross your fingers, you know.
And I lost courage.
And I went to a bar to have a little courage.
And I saw this lady sitting over there with her legs crossed and all.
She wasn't good looking or anything, but I started thinking, everybody is distracted by anything sexual.
So I got some of these papers that they have and tore the top off and all, and made three or four the most pornographic drawings I could think of.
And I knew that this would give me a reason to be confused.
So I hid them where I knew they would find them, of course.
And went back and, of course, sir-- boy, how they hated to say sir-- are you taking any-- especially to some dumb kid, you know-- are you taking any secret messages or any-- oh, no.
And well, would you mind opening this?
Well, I seem to-- oh, its doesn't matter.
Opened it.
And of course they found them.
He said, oh, you know, you can't take-- this is a Catholic country.
You can't take this into Spain.
And we'll have to confiscate them.
So they closed the bags, and I-- I went out.
I think that was my first real acting.
I started method acting.
I started saying, what am I supposed to be, a dog with his tail between his legs.
So I slunk out like this.

And as I got to the door to go through on the way to Spain, I looked back and they were passing them around and laughing and all, and all I wanted to do was jump in there and click my heels, and-- and-- then I went to Barcelona.
And I was coming out of-- I had some messages for the British consulate.
And I came out of the consulate and was arrested and taken into occupied France.
Some policemen.
And the French-- the Spaniards were not very-- individually, were not pro-Nazi.
But the-- officially they were supposed to cooperate a little bit.
But they did.
In this particular case, there were some who did.
We never knew who they were.
I don't know if they were Spaniards or what.
But anyway, I was waiting in this bench with several other people to be interrogated, with my bags there-- trumpet in a little bag there.
And I saw this officer coming out with a-- with a collaborator hanging on him, on his arm, you know, talking to him and trying to convince him of something.
And, you know, they hated the collaborators, as you can imagine, more than anything.
And so, I don't know what-- you know, adrenaline is a fantastic thing.
Suddenly I jumped up and opened the door for them.
And then I reached back and took my two cases, the trumpet and the case, and followed them out the door, and then out the courtyard, and then out the gate.
And everybody thought I was carrying their luggage.
Except they didn't, because they were talking.
And I was far enough behind them so that-- and I was also acting it up pretty well.
And when we got out, then I-- I hitchhiked later.
I got on the train first, and went to the end of the station-- of the train and got off before it started.
And then I-- because I knew they would be looking for me.
At least, I thought they would.
Maybe they thought, oh, this stupid kid, would could he-- anyway, eventually, I hitchhiked on a truck.
And the driver, the truck driver, hid me on his truck and took me into Spain.
Then everything was all right.
Then I got to Lisbon.
And that's-- then when I got there, I think I took about four baths the first day-- first night.
And I don't know how much room service and everything.
And then I went down to a cafe on-- underneath the Metropol hotel.
And there where a lot of our friends, the Jewish refugees, you know, it was like old home week.
And I saw a big sign with my name on it, Lillian Fawcett was going to give a concert for-- at the Theatre Trinidad.
And so the next night, couple of nights later, I go to this concert.
And there's this gorgeous lady with a wonderful voice.
She became very famous later.
She sang in the Hollywood Bowl.
And she doubled for-- she took the place of Lily Pons one time at the San Francisco Opera when she was ill.
And she had a big career.
But she was singing.
She recognized me.
Well, I was quite friendly with Mrs.
Pell, you know, Herbert Pell was our ambassador.
And I had met his wife quite by accident.
And she needed extra men-- extra men-- kids at-- at dinner and so forth.
And so she used to invite me quite often.
And her son is Claiborne Pell, you know, the senator.
And her painting name was Olive Biglow.
That was really how we got to be such good friends because she was such a good painter.
And I used to be extra man there at dinner.
About-- well, I was just waiting to go to England, to the RAF.
We've given up the idea of the free French, and I was going to go to the RAF.
But I ran into Mr.
Mitchell and Donald Darling who became-- I didn't know it at the time-- but he became very famous as a-- as an agent, you know.
And he-- when we would-- Mr.
Mitchell told us not-- he was head of Intelligence Service there-- told us not to ever be seen in public with him.
But Donald, and Bill Holland, and I used to go out together sometimes.
And one night we come home, and of course, there was our spy under the newspaper-- reading the newspaper under the light, like in a bad movie.
And we were a little bit plastered.
So Bill turns, good night Darling, good night Darling, you know.
That's his last name.
And you called each other that a lot.
From then on, they never bothered us.
They just thought they were-- we were two of the boys, you know. it was-- from then on we were in good, safe hands.
And then Mrs.
Pell said to me one day, you told me you were a bachelor.
And I said, I am.
She said, well, they told me at the-- at the consulate that a Mrs.
Charles Fawcett had applied for a visa.
I said, coincidence.
Well, the second coincidence happened a few days later.
And then she said, well, look, I think you had better go up to-- I shouldn't be saying this, because after all her husband was an ambassador.
You'd better go up to the-- to a Porto in the North and go to the consulate.
And I'll phone them and tell them you're coming.
And that way, you can-- they won't know about the one down there.
You can't imagine how she helped us, and all.
And that's where I took the girl that I had-- you know, the singer.
And--
Yes, yes.
